source_book: DC Pandey Class 12 Ch.4 Moving Charges and Magnetism §4.5 (Ampère's Circuital Law) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. NCERT consulted for the Indian real-world anchor only. The atomic concept being taught is Ampère's circuital law ∮B·dl = μ₀ I_enc applied to a long straight wire: choose a coaxial circular Amperian loop, use symmetry (|B| constant AND tangent on the loop) to reduce the line integral to ∮B·dl = B·(2πr), set it equal to μ₀ I_enc, and DERIVE B = μ₀I/(2πr). This is the integral-law route to the straight-wire field — the alternative to the Biot-Savart element-summation route (biot_savart_law), and it returns the SAME field. The deepest confusions are: students think any loop shape works, they treat ∮B·dl as un-evaluable symbolic calculus, and they conflate I_enc with a quantity that grows with the loop. The field_3d renderer makes the loop, the equal B·dl tiles, the accumulation, and the unroll-to-a-bar visible in 3D. WIRE ONLY — no solenoid, no toroid, no off-axis or non-symmetric loop. Conceptual-only ship (no mode_overrides). EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; misconceptions confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch + straightforward contrast beats). Rule 31 retrofit 2026-07-04: converted from the old Socratic model to straightforward distinct-motion beats with per-state contextual controls — no prediction pauses, glow bindings drive focus, STATE_1 exposes I only, STATE_6 exposes r only (the unrolled bar tracks 2πr live once grabbed), STATE_8 is the open explorer (all controls, duration 0).

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the thick overhead power transmission line on steel towers near a highway, humming with hundreds of amps. You cannot sum its magnetic field piece by piece in your head — but draw one invisible circle, centred on the wire and passing through your point, and Ampère's law hands you B = μ₀I/2πr directly. The circulation of B once around that loop equals μ₀ times the enclosed current, and symmetry does the rest.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture the heavy copper lead feeding a school or college physics-lab electromagnet. A large steady current runs through it. To find the field at a nearby point you do not integrate countless little contributions — you wrap a coaxial circle through the point, and the law converts the whole circulation into one clean equation B·(2πr) = μ₀I.

formulas.symmetry_reduction: ∮ B·dl = B · (2π r)   [|B| constant and B ∥ dl on the loop ⇒ cosθ = 1]
formulas.enclosed_current: I_enc = I   (one wire threads the loop)
formulas.tangency: B ∥ dl everywhere on a coaxial circle ⇒ B·dl = B dl,  cosθ = 1,  θ = 0°
formulas.direction: B circulates around the wire (right-hand grip rule); the loop is coaxial with the wire

constraints: The Amperian loop is a circle coaxial with the wire (centred on the wire, in the plane perpendicular to it)
|B| is constant at every point of this loop — this is what symmetry buys, and it is why a circle is chosen
B is tangent to the loop at every point: B ∥ dl, θ = 0°, cosθ = 1, so B·dl = B dl with no radial or axial part
I_enc = the total current threading the loop = I for a single enclosed wire; it does NOT depend on the loop radius
∮B·dl = B·(2πr) holds ONLY because |B| is constant and tangent — it is not true for an arbitrary loop shape
∮B·dl = μ₀ I_enc is exact and independent of loop radius: a bigger loop has larger 2πr but smaller B, product fixed at μ₀I
This concept applies to the long straight wire ONLY — no solenoid, no toroid, no off-axis or non-symmetric loop
The unrolled bar's length is the real magnitude N·B·dl = B·(2πr); emphasis is by brightness, never by a cosmetic size pulse (Rule 29)

aha_moment.statement: The circulation ∮B·dl is a finite sum of equal B·dl tiles around the loop.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: 24 equal tiles light in sequence round the coaxial circle and fill the accumulator ring — the integral assembled piece by equal piece — then unroll into one bar of length B·2πr.

STATE_1.title: The result you already know — but unearned

scene_composition.text: Straight wire: B = μ₀I / 2πr

You met this with
the compass

But where do the
2π and the r come from?

tts_sentences.text_en: You already know this: the magnetic field B = μ₀I over 2πr for a long straight wire. You met it with the compass.
tts_sentences.text_hi: आप यह पहले से जानते हैं: magnetic field B equals mu-zero I over two pi r, एक long straight wire के लिए। आप इसे compass के साथ मिल चुके हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: మీకు ఇది ఇప్పటికే తెలుసు: magnetic field B equals mu-zero I over two pi r, ఒక long straight wire కోసం. మీరు దీన్ని compass తో కలిశారు.

tts_sentences.text_en: But where do the 2π and the radius r come from? Why exactly that size?
tts_sentences.text_hi: लेकिन यह two pi और radius r कहाँ से आते हैं? ठीक उस size का क्यों?
tts_sentences.text_te: కానీ ఈ two pi మరియు radius r ఎక్కడ నుండి వస్తాయి? సరిగ్గా ఆ size ఎందుకు?

tts_sentences.text_en: We will not assume this formula. We will earn it — and it returns, lit, at the end.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हम इस formula को assume नहीं करेंगे। हम इसे earn करेंगे — और यह अंत में, रोशन होकर, वापस आएगा।
tts_sentences.text_te: మనం ఈ formula ని assume చేయము. మనం దాన్ని earn చేస్తాము — మరియు అది చివరలో, వెలిగిపోతూ, తిరిగి వస్తుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: B=μ₀I/2πr is just a formula to memorise
misconception_watch.visual_counter: the same formula returns lit in STATE_7 as the OUTPUT of the circulation, never assumed
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: 2πr is the circumference the field circulates around; it falls out of the loop, it is not put in by hand.

STATE_2.title: Ampère's law — circulation equals μ₀ I_enc

scene_composition.text: ∮ B·dl = μ₀ I_enc

∮ means: add B·dl
all the way round
one closed loop

I_enc = the current
threading the loop

tts_sentences.text_en: Ampère's law: the circulation of the magnetic field B once around a closed loop equals μ₀ times the enclosed current.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Ampère's law: किसी closed loop के चारों ओर magnetic field B की circulation, enclosed current के mu-zero गुना के बराबर होती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Ampère's law: ఒక closed loop చుట్టూ magnetic field B యొక్క circulation, enclosed current యొక్క mu-zero రెట్లకు సమానం.

tts_sentences.text_en: The circle on the integral means: add up B·dl all the way round one closed loop.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Integral पर circle का मतलब है: एक पूरे closed loop में B dot dl को जोड़ते जाइए।
tts_sentences.text_te: Integral పై circle అర్థం: ఒక పూర్తి closed loop చుట్టూ B dot dl ని కూడుతూ వెళ్ళండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: I-enclosed is the current threading the loop — our single wire passes straight through.
tts_sentences.text_hi: I-enclosed वह current है जो loop के अंदर से thread होती है — हमारा single wire सीधे उससे गुज़रता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: I-enclosed అనేది loop లోంచి thread అయ్యే current — మన single wire నేరుగా దాని గుండా వెళ్తుంది.

misconception_watch.visual_counter: a marker physically walks once round the loop, dropping a B·dl tile each step
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: ∮ means: add B·dl around one complete closed loop — a walk, not a mystery.

STATE_3.title: Why a circle, not any loop

scene_composition.text: Could ANY loop shape work?

Square: corners are
closer → B stronger
there → arrows unequal

Coaxial circle: every
point same distance →
|B| equal all the way round

tts_sentences.text_en: Could ANY loop shape work — say a square? Watch the square try.
tts_sentences.text_hi: क्या कोई भी loop shape काम कर सकती है — जैसे एक square? Square को देखिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఏదైనా loop shape పని చేస్తుందా — అంటే ఒక square? Square ని చూడండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: On a square the corners sit closer to the wire, so the magnetic field B is stronger there — the arrows are unequal.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक square पर corners wire के ज़्यादा करीब होते हैं, इसलिए वहाँ magnetic field B ज़्यादा strong होता है — arrows असमान होते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక square పై corners wire కి దగ్గరగా ఉంటాయి, కాబట్టి అక్కడ magnetic field B బలంగా ఉంటుంది — arrows అసమానంగా ఉంటాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Only a circle centred on the wire keeps every point the same distance — |B| equal all the way round.
tts_sentences.text_hi: सिर्फ wire पर centred circle ही हर point को समान distance पर रखता है — |B| पूरे रास्ते equal।
tts_sentences.text_te: Wire పై centred circle మాత్రమే ప్రతి point ని సమాన distance లో ఉంచుతుంది — |B| మొత్తం చుట్టూ equal.

misconception_watch.belief: the loop shape is free, so a square is fine for finding B
misconception_watch.visual_counter: square loop arrows drawn unequal in length; circle arrows all equal
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: any loop obeys the law, but only the coaxial circle has constant |B| — the condition that lets you SOLVE for B.

STATE_4.title: The angle between B and dl is zero

scene_composition.text:
B and dl — what is the
angle between them?

B is tangent, dl runs
along the circle →
same way → θ = 0°

Two gifts: |B| constant,
cosθ = 1 → B·dl = B dl

tts_sentences.text_en: At any point on the circle, watch the angle between the magnetic field B and the step dl.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Circle पर किसी भी point पर, magnetic field B और कदम dl के बीच के angle को देखिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: Circle పై ఏ point వద్దనైనా, magnetic field B మరియు అడుగు dl మధ్య angle ని చూడండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: The magnetic field B is tangent, and dl runs along the circle too — they point the same way. The angle is zero.
tts_sentences.text_hi: magnetic field B tangent है, और dl भी circle के साथ चलता है — दोनों एक ही direction में point करते हैं। angle zero है।
tts_sentences.text_te: magnetic field B tangent గా ఉంది, dl కూడా circle వెంట నడుస్తుంది — రెండూ ఒకే direction లో point చేస్తాయి. angle zero.

tts_sentences.text_en: Two gifts: |B| constant, and cosθ = 1 everywhere. So B·dl is simply B times dl.
tts_sentences.text_hi: दो तोहफे: |B| constant, और cosθ = 1 हर जगह। तो B·dl बस B times dl है।
tts_sentences.text_te: రెండు బహుమతులు: |B| constant, మరియు cosθ = 1 అన్ని చోట్లా. కాబట్టి B·dl అనేది simply B times dl.

misconception_watch.belief: B points partly radially / the dot product needs a cosθ that changes round the loop
misconception_watch.visual_counter: B drawn tangent (θ=0°, cosθ=1) at every sampled point; no radial component exists
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: B is purely tangent on a coaxial circle, so B·dl = B·dl everywhere — the dot product is trivial.

STATE_5.title: The circulation is a sum of equal tiles

scene_composition.text:
∮B·dl = a sum of
equal pieces

24 equal B·dl steps —
watch the ring fill,
piece by equal piece

the filled ring IS the
circulation ∮B·dl

tts_sentences.text_en: So the integral is not mysterious. It is a sum of equal pieces. Watch one full trip round the loop.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो यह integral कोई रहस्यमय चीज़ नहीं है। यह बराबर pieces का एक sum है। देखिए loop के चारों तरफ एक पूरा चक्कर।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి ఈ integral ఏమీ mysterious కాదు. ఇది సమాన pieces యొక్క sum. loop చుట్టూ ఒక పూర్తి trip గమనించండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Each little step contributes the same B·dl — and there are 24 equal steps. See the ring fill, piece by equal piece.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हर छोटा step एक जैसा B·dl contribute करता है — और यहाँ 24 equal steps हैं। देखिए ring कैसे piece by equal piece भरती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ప్రతి చిన్న step అదే B·dl ని contribute చేస్తుంది — మరియు 24 equal steps ఉన్నాయి. ring ఎలా piece by equal piece నిండుతుందో చూడండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: That filled ring IS the circulation ∮B·dl — assembled, not hand-waved. A finite sum of equal tiles.
tts_sentences.text_hi: वह भरी हुई ring ही circulation ∮B·dl है — assembled है, hand-waved नहीं। equal tiles का एक finite sum।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఆ నిండిన ring నే circulation ∮B·dl — assembled గా తయారైంది, hand-waved కాదు. సమాన tiles యొక్క ఒక finite sum.

misconception_watch.belief: the circulation integral can't really be evaluated / it's just symbolic
misconception_watch.visual_counter: 24 equal B·dl tiles accumulate round the loop into a filled ring = the circulation
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: ∮B·dl is a finite, concrete sum of equal tiles because |B| and the angle are constant on the circle.

scene_composition.text:
Straighten the ring out —
no length added or lost

24 equal tiles line up
into one bar — length
= once round = 2πr

∮B·dl = B × 2πr
(a clean product)

tts_sentences.text_en: Now straighten that ring out. The same 24 equal tiles line up into one bar — no length is added or lost.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब उस ring को सीधा कीजिए। वही 24 equal tiles एक bar में लाइन हो जाते हैं — कोई length जोड़ी या घटाई नहीं गई।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు ఆ ring ని straight చేయండి. అదే 24 equal tiles ఒక bar లో line అవుతాయి — ఎటువంటి length జోడించబడలేదు లేదా తగ్గించబడలేదు.

tts_sentences.text_en: The bar's length is just the number of steps times their size — which is the full distance once around: 2πr.
tts_sentences.text_hi: उस bar की length बस steps की संख्या गुना उनका size है — जो कि पूरे एक चक्कर की distance है: 2πr।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఆ bar యొక్క length అనేది steps సంఖ్య times వాటి size మాత్రమే — అది ఒక పూర్తి చుట్టు distance: 2πr.

tts_sentences.text_en: So the circular sum becomes a clean product: ∮B·dl = B times 2πr.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो circular sum एक साफ़ product बन जाता है: ∮B·dl = B times 2πr।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి circular sum ఒక clean product గా మారుతుంది: ∮B·dl = B times 2πr.

misconception_watch.belief: circulation is a complicated curved sum, not a clean product
misconception_watch.visual_counter: the ring unrolls to a straight bar of measured length 2πr; ∮B·dl = B·(2πr)
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: because every tile is equal, the circular sum is just (one tile) × (number round) = B × circumference = B·2πr.

STATE_7.title: Set equal to μ₀I, solve for B

scene_composition.text: B·(2πr) = μ₀ I_enc

one wire → I_enc = I
divide both sides by 2πr

B = μ₀I / 2πr —
the first screen, earned

tts_sentences.text_en: Ampère's law set that circulation equal to μ₀ I-enclosed. For our one wire, I-enclosed is just the current I.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Ampère's law उस circulation को μ₀ I-enclosed के बराबर रखता है। हमारे एक wire के लिए, I-enclosed बस current I है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Ampère's law ఆ circulation ని μ₀ I-enclosed కి సమానంగా చేస్తుంది. మన ఒక wire కోసం, I-enclosed అనేది కేవలం current I మాత్రమే.

tts_sentences.text_en: So B times 2πr equals μ₀ I. One last move gets the magnetic field B alone.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो B times 2πr equals μ₀ I। magnetic field B को अकेले पाने के लिए एक आखिरी कदम बचा है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి B times 2πr equals μ₀ I. magnetic field B ని వేరుగా పొందడానికి ఒక చివరి move మిగిలింది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Divide both sides by 2πr. The magnetic field B = μ₀ I over 2πr — the exact formula from the very first screen, now earned.
tts_sentences.text_hi: दोनों sides को 2πr से divide कीजिए। magnetic field B = μ₀ I over 2πr — यही exact formula पहली screen से था, और अब हमने इसे earn किया है।
tts_sentences.text_te: రెండు sides ని 2πr తో divide చేయండి. magnetic field B = μ₀ I over 2πr — మొదటి screen నుండి ఉన్న exact formula ఇదే, ఇప్పుడు మనం దాన్ని సాధించాం.

misconception_watch.belief: Biot-Savart and Ampère give different B
misconception_watch.visual_counter: the earned B = μ₀I/2πr re-lights STATE_1's faint formula exactly — same value
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Ampère's law and Biot-Savart give the IDENTICAL field for a straight wire; they are two routes to one answer.

STATE_8.title: Your turn — change I and r, watch the law hold

scene_composition.text: Slide I and the loop radius r

scene_composition.label: I — current; I_enc and B both rise with it
r — loop radius; 2πr grows, I_enc holds, B falls 1/r

scene_composition.text: μ₀I is fixed → B·2πr never changes → B falls as 1/r

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn. Slide the current I up — I-enclosed rises, and the magnetic field B rises with it.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब आपकी बारी है। current I को ऊपर slide कीजिए — I-enclosed बढ़ता है, और magnetic field B उसके साथ बढ़ती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: మీ వంతు. current I ని పైకి slide చేయండి — I-enclosed పెరుగుతుంది, magnetic field B దానితో పాటు పెరుగుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Grow the loop radius r — 2πr gets longer, but I-enclosed stays fixed: a bigger loop encloses no extra current.
tts_sentences.text_hi: loop का radius r बढ़ाइए — 2πr लंबा हो जाता है, लेकिन I-enclosed fixed रहता है: एक बड़ा loop कोई extra current enclose नहीं करता।
tts_sentences.text_te: loop radius r ని పెంచండి — 2πr పొడవు అవుతుంది, కానీ I-enclosed fixed గా ఉంటుంది: పెద్ద loop ఏ extra current ని enclose చేయదు.

tts_sentences.text_en: μ₀I is fixed, so as 2πr grows the magnetic field B falls as one over r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: μ₀I fixed है, इसलिए जैसे 2πr बढ़ता है, magnetic field B one over r की तरह fall होती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: μ₀I fixed, కాబట్టి 2πr పెరిగే కొద్దీ magnetic field B one over r గా fall అవుతుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: a bigger Amperian loop encloses more current / changes the law
misconception_watch.visual_counter: as r grows, I_enc readout holds at I while 2πr grows and B falls 1/r
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: the loop's size never changes I_enc for a single wire — it only changes the circumference the field is spread around.

STATE_1.label: The result you already know — but unearned

tts_sentences.text_en: You already know this: B = μ₀I over 2πr for a long straight wire. You met it with the compass.
But where do the 2π and the r come from? Why exactly that size?
We will not assume this formula. We will earn it — and it returns, lit, at the end.

STATE_2.label: Ampère's law — circulation equals μ₀ I_enc

tts_sentences.text_en: Ampère's law: the circulation of B once around a closed loop equals μ₀ times the enclosed current.
The circle on the integral means: add up B·dl all the way round one closed loop.
I-enclosed is the current threading the loop — our single wire passes straight through.

STATE_3.label: Why a circle, not any loop

tts_sentences.text_en: Could ANY loop shape work — say a square? Watch the square try.
On a square the corners sit closer to the wire, so B is stronger there — the arrows are unequal.
Only a circle centred on the wire keeps every point the same distance — |B| equal all the way round.

STATE_4.label: The angle between B and dl is zero

tts_sentences.text_en: At any point on the circle, watch the angle between B and the step dl.
B is tangent, and dl runs along the circle too — they point the same way. The angle is zero.
Two gifts: |B| constant, and cosθ = 1 everywhere. So B·dl is simply B times dl.

STATE_5.label: The circulation is a sum of equal tiles

tts_sentences.text_en: So the integral is not mysterious. It is a sum of equal pieces. Watch one full trip round the loop.
Each little step contributes the same B·dl — and there are 24 equal steps. See the ring fill, piece by equal piece.
That filled ring IS the circulation ∮B·dl — assembled, not hand-waved. A finite sum of equal tiles.
Now straighten that ring out. The same 24 equal tiles line up into one bar — no length is added or lost.
The bar's length is just the number of steps times their size — which is the full distance once around: 2πr.
So the circular sum becomes a clean product: ∮B·dl = B times 2πr.

STATE_7.label: Set equal to μ₀I, solve for B

tts_sentences.text_en: Ampère's law set that circulation equal to μ₀ I-enclosed. For our one wire, I-enclosed is just I.
So B times 2πr equals μ₀ I. One last move gets B alone.
Divide both sides by 2πr. B = μ₀ I over 2πr — the exact formula from the very first screen, now earned.

STATE_8.label: Your turn — change I and r, watch the law hold

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn. Slide the current I up — I-enclosed rises, and B rises with it.
Grow the loop radius r — 2πr gets longer, but I-enclosed stays fixed: a bigger loop encloses no extra current.
μ₀I is fixed, so as 2πr grows B falls as one over r.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered Ampère's circuital law for a long straight wire when they can state that ∮B·dl = μ₀ I_enc, that I_enc is the TOTAL current threading the Amperian loop (= I for a single enclosed wire, and independent of the loop's size), that a CIRCLE coaxial with the wire is chosen because only then is |B| the same at every point so B can be factored out of the integral, that B is tangent to that loop so the dot product B·dl reduces to B·dl with θ = 0° and cosθ = 1, that the circulation is therefore a finite sum of equal B·dl tiles which unrolls to the clean product B·(2πr), and that setting B·(2πr) = μ₀I and dividing by 2πr DERIVES B = μ₀I/(2πr) — the identical field the Biot-Savart route gives. They should also see that doubling the loop radius leaves I_enc unchanged while B falls as 1/r, because the product B·(2πr) = μ₀I is fixed. (The solenoid, toroid, and non-symmetric-loop cases are downstream applications, not assessed by this conceptual V1 quiz.)

questions.stem: A long straight wire carries current I. You draw a circular Amperian loop around it. In ∮B·dl = μ₀ I_enc, what is I_enc?

options.A: The total current I in the wire
options.B: Half of I, since the loop is on one side
options.C: I multiplied by the loop radius r
options.D: Zero, because the wire is outside the loop

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks the loop only sees part of the current geometrically
distractor_misconceptions.C: Confuses I_enc with a quantity that scales with loop size
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks a coaxial loop does not enclose the wire it surrounds

questions.tested_idea: I_enc is the total current threading the loop
questions.parallel_form_stem: A wire carries 8 A. A circular Amperian loop is drawn coaxially around it. What current does the loop enclose?

questions.stem: Why is a CIRCLE (coaxial with the wire) chosen as the Amperian loop, rather than a square?

options.A: Only on the circle is |B| the same at every point, so B can be factored out of the integral
options.B: A circle is easier to draw than a square
options.C: Magnetic field only exists in circular shapes
options.D: A square loop would enclose a different current

distractor_misconceptions.B: Treats loop choice as convenience, not symmetry-driven
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks B is geometrically circular so the loop must be too
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks loop shape changes I_enc (it does not)

questions.tested_idea: Constant-|B| symmetry is what makes the loop choice work
questions.parallel_form_stem: On a square Amperian loop around a straight wire, why can't you simply pull B outside the integral ∮B·dl?

questions.stem: On the circular loop, the dot product B·dl simplifies to just B·dl (no cosine factor). Why?

options.A: B is tangent to the circle and dl runs along the circle, so the angle between them is 0° (cosθ = 1)
options.B: Because B is very small so the angle doesn't matter
options.C: Because dl is always perpendicular to B

distractor_misconceptions.B: Hand-waves the dot product instead of using θ=0°
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks B is radial / perpendicular to the loop
distractor_misconceptions.D: Confuses steady current with the geometry of the dot product

questions.tested_idea: B is tangent to the loop ⇒ θ=0°, cosθ=1
questions.parallel_form_stem: At a point on the circular Amperian loop, what is the angle between the magnetic field B and the path element dl?

questions.stem: The circulation ∮B·dl around the loop is best understood as:

options.A: A sum of many equal contributions B·dl, one for each equal step around the loop
options.B: A single value you must memorise
options.C: The area enclosed by the loop
options.D: Different at each point so it cannot be added up

distractor_misconceptions.C: Confuses circulation (a line integral) with enclosed area
distractor_misconceptions.D: Doesn't realise B·dl is equal at every point

questions.tested_idea: Circulation is a finite sum of equal B·dl tiles (THE AHA)
questions.parallel_form_stem: Walking once around the coaxial circle, each equal step contributes the same B·dl. What does adding all these contributions give?

questions.stem: Using |B| constant and tangent on the loop, ∮B·dl becomes B·(2πr). Setting this equal to μ₀I_enc and solving for B gives:

distractor_misconceptions.D: Confuses Ampère's result with the Biot-Savart 1/r² element form

questions.parallel_form_stem: From B·(2πr) = μ₀I, isolate B by dividing both sides by 2πr. What is the result?

questions.stem: You double the radius of the Amperian loop (keeping the same wire and current). What happens to I_enc and to B at the loop?

options.A: I_enc stays the same; B becomes half as large
options.C: I_enc doubles; B stays the same

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks a bigger loop encloses more current
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks I_enc grows with loop size and B is unaffected
distractor_misconceptions.D: Doesn't see the 1/r dependence of B

questions.tested_idea: Loop size does not change I_enc; B falls as 1/r
questions.parallel_form_stem: A larger coaxial Amperian loop is drawn around the same wire. How do the enclosed current and the field on the loop compare?
